facts.t: President William McKinley is assassinated at the Pan-American Exposition; Theodore Roosevelt becomes the youngest president.
The Spindletop oil gusher erupts in Texas, kicking off the American oil boom.
J.P. Morgan forms U.S. Steel, the world's first billion-dollar corporation.
King Camp Gillette founds his safety razor company, changing how America shaves.
The American League is organized as a Major League, rivaling the National League.
Theodore Roosevelt personally mediates the Anthracite Coal Strike, the first presidential intervention in a labor dispute.
The teddy bear is named after Roosevelt's refusal to shoot a captured bear on a Mississippi hunting trip.
The first Rose Bowl game is played; Michigan crushes Stanford 49-0.
Cadillac Motor Car Company is founded in Detroit.
Temperance crusader Carrie Nation keeps smashing up saloons with her hatchet.
The Wright brothers make the first powered airplane flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.
Henry Ford founds the Ford Motor Company in Detroit.
The first World Series is played; the Boston Americans beat the Pittsburgh Pirates.
'The Great Train Robbery' becomes one of the first films to tell a story with editing and continuity.
Caleb Bradham registers the Pepsi-Cola trademark.
St. Louis hosts the 1904 Summer Olympics, the first Olympic Games held in the United States.
New York City opens its first subway line.
Jack Daniel's Tennessee whiskey wins a gold medal at the St. Louis World's Fair.
Ice cream cones are popularized at the St. Louis World's Fair.
Theodore Roosevelt wins election to a full term as president in a landslide.
Theodore Roosevelt brokers the Treaty of Portsmouth, ending the Russo-Japanese War.
The radical Industrial Workers of the World union is founded in Chicago.
Ty Cobb makes his major league debut with the Detroit Tigers.
The first Rotary Club, a civic service organization, is founded in Chicago.
Variety, the show-business trade magazine, publishes its first issue.
The 1906 San Francisco earthquake and fire kill thousands and level much of the city.
Upton Sinclair publishes 'The Jungle,' exposing filthy conditions in the meatpacking industry.
The Pure Food and Drug Act becomes law, creating federal oversight of food and medicine.
Theodore Roosevelt becomes the first American to win the Nobel Peace Prize.
The Antiquities Act lets Roosevelt declare Devils Tower the first US National Monument.
The Panic of 1907 rattles Wall Street until J.P. Morgan personally organizes a bailout.
Oklahoma is admitted to the Union as the 46th state.
Florenz Ziegfeld stages the first Ziegfeld Follies revue in New York.
Metered, gasoline-powered taxicabs hit the streets of New York City for the first time.
A grand new hotel, the Plaza, opens on Fifth Avenue in New York City.
Ford Motor Company introduces the Model T, the car that will put America on wheels.
The Bureau of Investigation, forerunner of the FBI, is founded.
The Chicago Cubs win the World Series, a title they will not repeat for over a century.
William Durant founds General Motors.
The Great White Fleet, sixteen US battleships, sails around the world to show off American naval power.
The NAACP is founded to fight for the civil rights of Black Americans.
The Lincoln cent debuts, the first US coin to feature an actual person's portrait.
Explorer Robert Peary claims to have reached the North Pole.
Alice Ramsey becomes the first woman to drive an automobile across the United States.
The Army takes delivery of the Wright Military Flyer, the first military airplane.
The Boy Scouts of America is founded.
Halley's Comet's return sparks public panic and a run on gas masks and 'comet pills.'
The first Father's Day is celebrated in Spokane, Washington.
The Mann Act makes it a federal crime to transport women across state lines for 'immoral purposes.'
A bomb destroys the Los Angeles Times building amid a bitter labor dispute, killing 21.
The Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire kills 146 workers in New York, spurring sweeping labor reforms.
The Supreme Court orders Standard Oil broken up as an illegal monopoly.
The first Indianapolis 500 auto race is held.
IBM's predecessor, the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company, is founded.
Future president Ronald Reagan is born in Tampico, Illinois.
The RMS Titanic sinks in the North Atlantic after striking an iceberg, killing over 1,500.
Arizona and New Mexico become the last states admitted in the contiguous United States.
The Oreo cookie goes on sale for the first time.
Fenway Park opens in Boston.
Theodore Roosevelt splits the Republican vote by running as the Bull Moose Progressive candidate.
The Federal Reserve Act creates the US central banking system.
The 16th Amendment is ratified, authorizing a federal income tax.
Ford introduces the moving assembly line, transforming car manufacturing.
Grand Central Terminal opens in New York City.
The Webb-Kenyon Act bans shipping liquor into 'dry' states, a milestone for the Prohibition movement.
The Panama Canal opens to shipping traffic.
World War I erupts in Europe; the United States declares neutrality.
Colorado militia and mine guards kill striking coal miners and their families in the Ludlow Massacre.
The Federal Trade Commission is established to police unfair business practices.
Babe Ruth makes his major league debut with the Boston Red Sox.
A German U-boat sinks the RMS Lusitania, killing 128 Americans and inflaming US opinion against Germany.
Alexander Graham Bell makes the first US transcontinental telephone call, to Thomas Watson in San Francisco.
'The Birth of a Nation' pioneers new film techniques while glorifying the Ku Klux Klan.
San Francisco hosts the Panama-Pacific International Exposition.
Coca-Cola patents its distinctive curved contour bottle.
Pancho Villa raids Columbus, New Mexico, prompting a US military expedition into Mexico.
The National Park Service is created to oversee America's national parks.
Jeannette Rankin is elected the first woman in the US Congress.
Boeing is founded in Seattle as the Pacific Aero Products Company.
Margaret Sanger opens the first US birth control clinic in Brooklyn.
The United States declares war on Germany, entering World War I.
The Selective Service Act reinstates the military draft.
The Jones-Shafroth Act grants US citizenship to residents of Puerto Rico.
The Espionage Act makes it a crime to interfere with US military operations.
Congress bans using grain to distill liquor as a wartime conservation measure.
World War I ends as the Armistice takes effect on November 11.
The deadly 1918 influenza pandemic begins its global spread.
The United States observes Daylight Saving Time for the first time.
The Wartime Prohibition Act bans strong alcoholic beverages ahead of full Prohibition.
The Boston Red Sox win the World Series, a title they won't repeat for 86 years.
The 18th Amendment is ratified, mandating nationwide Prohibition starting in 1920.
Chicago 'Black Sox' players conspire to throw the World Series for gambling money.
A burst molasses tank floods Boston streets with a wave of syrup, killing 21.
Congress passes the 19th Amendment, sending women's suffrage to the states.
The Treaty of Versailles is signed, formally ending World War I.
The 19th Amendment is ratified, guaranteeing American women the right to vote.
Prohibition begins nationwide as the Volstead Act takes effect.
KDKA in Pittsburgh makes the first commercial radio broadcast in the US.
The Negro National League is founded, the first successful Black professional baseball league.
A bomb explodes on Wall Street, killing 38 in an unsolved anarchist attack.
White mobs destroy Tulsa's Greenwood district in the Tulsa Race Massacre, killing as many as 300 people.
The Emergency Quota Act imposes the first strict numerical limits on immigration.
The first Miss America pageant is held in Atlantic City.
Babe Ruth hits 59 home runs, breaking his own single-season record.
West Virginia coal miners and lawmen clash in the Battle of Blair Mountain, one of the largest labor uprisings in US history.
The Teapot Dome scandal begins to surface, exposing bribery over federal oil leases.
The Lincoln Memorial is dedicated in Washington, D.C.
Reader's Digest magazine publishes its first issue.
Emily Post publishes 'Etiquette,' becoming America's authority on manners.
Silent-film star Fatty Arbuckle is acquitted in a manslaughter case that scandalized Hollywood.
President Warren G. Harding dies suddenly; Calvin Coolidge is sworn in.
Yankee Stadium, 'The House That Ruth Built,' opens in the Bronx.
Time magazine publishes its first issue.
The Hollywoodland sign is erected in the hills above Los Angeles.
The Charleston dance craze sweeps the country after debuting in a Broadway revue.
The Immigration Act of 1924 sets strict national quotas, sharply cutting immigration.
The Indian Citizenship Act grants US citizenship to Native Americans born in the country.
George Gershwin premieres 'Rhapsody in Blue' in New York.
J. Edgar Hoover is named director of the Bureau of Investigation, later the FBI.
Leopold and Loeb murder a Chicago boy in a case dubbed the 'crime of the century.'
The Scopes 'Monkey' Trial in Tennessee puts the teaching of evolution on trial.
F. Scott Fitzgerald publishes 'The Great Gatsby,' capturing Jazz Age bootlegger excess.
The New Yorker magazine publishes its first issue.
Nellie Tayloe Ross is sworn in as governor of Wyoming, the first woman to lead a US state.
Walter Chrysler founds the Chrysler Corporation.
